verbatim_text: Great question, actually. Okay, let me repeat it. So the point is like how does the surrogate reward helping with exploration at test time, right? If I understand correctly. I think if you look at like, you know, what the terminal reward does, you're only rewarding it based on correctness. So if you look at the correlation, so if the model has a prior to produce a proof for a given theorem, that's typically what the models do. You give it a theorem, it tries to produce a proof. And now if it immediately sees terminal rewards of one for that proof, that's what it ends up learning. If it did not see any rewards, there is no learning signal. Now, if it saw a reward wherein it tried to make a proof attempt and like, you know, if that was incorrect, it tried to reattempt and that's where it saw a reward, then that's the behavior that surrogate rewards amplify. So you can think about surrogate rewards amplifying in context exploration, wherein you're making a proof attempt, you're trying to revise it, sort of like how you like, you know, solve a particular problem. So previously we were only rewarding it based on correctness. Now, there is a version wherein you can reward it on just correctness and maybe the model has enough priors that it can associate like, you know, different like strategies like, you know, trying a proof, trying a different proof and doing in context exploration with that terminal reward. But building that correlation is very like hard in general with the right priors.
